Moreover, if we look at this segment as a whole, everyone is dealing with weaker demand for EVs, forecasts are being cut dramatically, and plans are being reevaluated.

For those who argue that Tesla should not be viewed as a traditional car manufacturer but as an AI company, fine. Let's compare it with the real titans of this sector. Nvidia (NVDA) currently has a forward P/E ratio of 31x-39x. Revenue growth and margins are excellent here, and FCF is also very strong. Then there is Microsoft (MSFT), which has a forward P/E ratio of approximately 33x and an EV/Sales ratio of 13x-14x. We are talking about stable double-digit growth, which Microsoft is delivering. In addition, it maintains gross margins of around 69%, and the company has massive FCF. Let's take a look at Apple (AAPL), about which I recently wrote an article saying that it is also overvalued, and yet compared to Tesla, it has a ridiculous valuation. Apple has a FWD P/E of around 32x, EV/EBITDA of 25x, and EV/Sales somewhere between 8x and 9x. It is also a premium company with a high moat, but even so, its valuation multiples are a third of those of Tesla.

So Tesla is overpriced even compared to the most expensive AI leaders. Its competitors have higher margins and better FCF, yet they trade at fractions of FWD P/E. If Tesla were to be valued at current multiples, it would have to deliver corresponding growth, which I do not see at this time.

Whether I look at Tesla through the lens of an AI player or an automaker (where it belongs), the probability of a price decline is higher than that of further growth. Tesla charges a luxury AI premium, but so far it can only deliver automotive results. Such a combination has no chance of lasting, especially in an environment of market peaks, a weakening labor market, and sticky inflation.

What could disprove my thesis, and what could prove it

If FSD/Robotaxi revenues were to be reported, the current valuations could start to make more sense. If Tesla launches paid rides in at least a few cities, obtains the necessary regulatory approvals, and demonstrates tangible APRU (stock $ per car per month), then it may be able to maintain its AI premium even without a rapid recovery in automotive margins.

Furthermore, Tesla could accelerate sales on Megapack. If NACS (network revenue) were also monetized, FCF could stabilize and offset weaker margins from Automotive. Indices are at highs, unemployment is rising, and inflation remains at higher levels. However, this does not mean that risk-on will break. The market can be irrational for a very long time, and we may run out of resources sooner. Here, it will be important to monitor Payrolls, JOLTS, unemployment, and Fed comments. If there is a hesitation, the multiples of hyper-valued companies will plummet. If demand recovers in Europe or China and discounts decline, we may also see further growth in share prices. Further regulatory credits could ease pressure on ASP, gross margins, and FCF. In such an environment, Tesla would be able to maintain its valuations and continue with its AI player premium.

How I See it

I don't want more fairy tales about a bright future, I want numbers. But nothing about Tesla makes sense to me. As far as I'm concerned, it's still a car manufacturer, sales are down, discounts are eating into margins, and deliveries are not on the rise. The Energy segment looks much better, but it is small compared to the automotive segment. FSD and robots are still just promises, with no results visible in the accounts, and their development is increasing costs.

The company's valuation is unrealistic. The market values Tesla as a winner in the AI segment, but so far it has only been able to deliver average numbers from the automotive segment. Moreover, the current macro environment does not help the current valuation at all. Indices are at new highs, the labor market is weakening, and inflation remains high. In such an environment, overvalued growth stocks, which Tesla definitely belongs to, fall the most. Unfortunately, I cannot take Musk's billion-dollar purchase as a signal either. If I compare it to the huge bonuses, it is just another PR stunt for me, not a change in fundamentals.

If there were real revenues from FSD or robots, or a visible jump in the Energy segment, then I might reconsider my conclusion. Until then, I will maintain my Strong Sell rating, which for me means shorting Tesla.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
